# Failure Learning Process Gap

## What I'm Seeing

`tasks/failed/self-review-loop.md` documents a task that scored 7/10 — all artifacts were well-crafted but the `agents.yaml` entry couldn't be added due to a READ-ONLY constraint conflict. This is a genuine learning: when a spec requires modifying a file an agent can't write to, the agent should surface this early (which Builder did correctly) and a failure learning should capture the pattern for future reference.

Yet `learnings/failures/` is empty. No agent wrote a learning about this failure. The `shared/self-improvement` skill explicitly says to write a learning when "a tool call fails unexpectedly" or when encountering a pattern that "suggests a systemic fix." The constraint conflict between spec requirements and agent access boundaries is exactly such a pattern — it will recur whenever a spec requires changes to `agents.yaml`, `universe/`, or other read-only artifacts.

Additionally, the blocker has since been resolved — the human added the reviewer entry to `agents.yaml`. The task in `tasks/failed/` may now be re-verifiable, but there's no convention for moving a failed task back through the pipeline after the blocker is resolved.

This touches three values from `universe/values.md`: "Every observation is potentially valuable" (the insight about access-boundary conflicts is being lost), "Nothing disappears" (the learning should exist as a record), and "What you learn alone is only valuable when others can learn it too" (Builder's insight about the constraint pattern isn't shared).

## What I Want to See

Two things: (1) A convention for what happens when a failed task's blocker is resolved — does it return to `tasks/review/` for re-verification, or does the human re-trigger the verifier? (2) A convention (or enforcement in the runtime) that failed verifications always produce a corresponding entry in `learnings/failures/` — either by Verifier writing it as part of the failure flow, or by Builder writing it when informed of the failure.
